content.context:
Read the text:
"Artificial Intelligence is reshaping the labour market faster than any previous technological revolution. While some predict mass unemployment, most labour economists paint a more nuanced picture. The World Economic Forum estimates that by 2027, AI will displace roughly 85 million jobs globally — yet simultaneously create 97 million new ones. The catch? The roles emerging (AI ethicists, prompt engineers, data curators) typically require retraining, which workers over 50 may struggle to access. Governments in Scandinavia have launched state-funded 'lifelong learning accounts', while countries lacking such safety nets could see widening inequality. As one analyst put it: 'The question is not whether AI takes jobs, but whether societies invest enough in helping people transition.'"

subQuestions.text: According to the WEF forecast, the NET effect on jobs by 2027 will be:

options.text: Gain of about 12 million jobs (more created than lost)

subQuestions.text: The main challenge for older workers is:
What solution do Scandinavian countries offer?
Assess.

statements.text: The text presents AI's impact on jobs as clearly catastrophic.
The author suggests that government policy plays a key role in how the AI transition affects people.

explanation: -85M + 97M = +12M net. Retraining dla starszych. Scandinavia = lifelong learning. Tekst wyważony, nie katastroficzny.

content.context:
Read the review:
"MOUNTAIN VIEW HOTEL ★★★☆☆
Rating: 3 out of 5

The location is spectacular — right at the foot of the mountains, with breathtaking views from every window. The staff were genuinely friendly and helpful, especially the receptionist who arranged a last-minute hiking tour for us.

However, the rooms desperately need an upgrade: the bathroom tiles were cracked, and the heating system was loud enough to wake us up twice during the night. Breakfast was basic — coffee, bread, some cold cuts — nothing impressive for the £140/night price tag.

Would I go back? Probably not, unless there was a major renovation. Beautiful setting, but the hotel itself doesn't live up to it. Recommended ONLY if you value location over comfort."

subQuestions.text: Summarize the reviewer's MAIN complaint in one English sentence.
subQuestions.sampleAnswer: The rooms and amenities are outdated and of poor quality, which doesn't match the high price.

subQuestions.text: Assess the review.

statements.text: The location is praised highly.
The reviewer complains about unfriendly staff.
The price is considered reasonable for what's offered.
The reviewer would likely return next year.

explanation: Pozytywne: lokalizacja, obsługa. Negatywne: pokoje, śniadanie, cena. Nie wróci bez renowacji.

content.context:
Read the text:
"'Digital detox' retreats — holidays where participants voluntarily surrender their smartphones and laptops — have become a multi-million-pound industry. Centres in Bali, the Scottish Highlands and Portugal now offer weeks without screens, where guests pay up to £3,000 for the privilege of forced disconnection. Participants often report improved sleep, reduced anxiety, and a reawakened ability to focus on single tasks. Yet psychologists warn that short-term retreats may offer little long-term benefit if underlying digital habits remain unchanged. 'It's like going on a crash diet,' says Dr Martha Collins of Oxford University. 'You feel better for a week, but you return to old patterns unless something fundamentally shifts.' A more sustainable approach, she argues, is gradual reduction combined with mindful use — a less dramatic but more durable solution."

subQuestions.text: In your own words (English), explain what a 'digital detox' retreat involves.
subQuestions.sampleAnswer: A digital detox retreat is a holiday where participants voluntarily give up their phones, laptops and other screens for a period of time, usually to reduce stress and improve wellbeing.

subQuestions.text: What is Dr Collins's main criticism of digital detox retreats?
Assess.

statements.text: Digital detox retreats can cost up to £3,000.
Dr Collins recommends quitting phones completely overnight.
The text only presents positive aspects of digital detoxes.
Mindful use and gradual reduction is suggested as a better alternative.

explanation: Up to £3,000, Bali/Scotland/Portugal. Crash diet analogy — nie trwałe. Rozwiązanie: stopniowa redukcja + mindful use.

content.context:
Read the text:
"The four-day work week, once dismissed as utopian, is gaining serious traction. Iceland's 2015–2019 trial — the largest ever conducted — involved 2,500 workers moving from 40 to 35-36 hours per week with no pay cut. The results were striking: productivity either remained the same or increased, while employee burnout dropped significantly. Following this, Iceland now has 86% of its workforce either on reduced hours or with the right to negotiate for them. However, sceptics warn that what works in a small, wealthy Nordic country may not translate to larger economies with different labour cultures. Moreover, some sectors — healthcare, emergency services, retail — face inherent difficulties in reducing hours without compromising service quality."

subQuestions.text: Explain (in English) what Iceland's trial involved.
subQuestions.sampleAnswer: Iceland ran a large trial between 2015 and 2019 where 2,500 workers reduced their working hours from 40 to 35-36 per week without any pay cut, to test if this would affect productivity and wellbeing.

subQuestions.text: What percentage of Iceland's workforce now benefits from shorter or negotiable hours?
Assess.

statements.text: Productivity dropped when working hours were reduced.
The text acknowledges that some sectors struggle to adopt a 4-day week.
The author presents the Icelandic trial as universally applicable.
Worker burnout decreased during the trial.

explanation: 2,500 workers, 40→35-36h, bez cięcia płac. Produktywność ta sama lub ↑, burnout ↓. 86% teraz. Sceptycy: NHS/retail mają problem.

content.context:
Read the text:
"Urban gardening — growing food on balconies, rooftops, and in tiny city flats — has exploded in popularity since 2020. A London-based study found that residents who started growing their own herbs and vegetables reported a 40% reduction in stress levels and saved an average of £30 per month on groceries. The trend goes beyond economics, though: urban gardeners often cite a sense of connection to nature and the satisfaction of 'watching something grow' as the main reasons they persist. The main challenges? Limited space, lack of sunlight, and — surprisingly — pigeons, who according to the study are the single biggest threat to urban balcony crops."

subQuestions.text: In English, explain what 'urban gardening' is according to the text.
subQuestions.sampleAnswer: Urban gardening means growing your own food (herbs, vegetables) in city environments — on balconies, rooftops, or in small apartments.

subQuestions.text: What financial benefit was reported?
Assess.

statements.text: The main motivation for urban gardeners is financial.
Pigeons are mentioned as a serious obstacle.
Urban gardening was popular long before 2020 according to the text.

explanation: 40% stress reduction, £30/month. Motywacja: połączenie z naturą (nie głównie finanse). Gołębie = największe zagrożenie.

content.context:
Read the text:
"Minimalism — the practice of living with fewer possessions — has gained popularity in recent years. Supporters claim it reduces stress, saves money, and frees up time for meaningful experiences. A recent survey of 2,000 adults who adopted a minimalist lifestyle for one year showed that 78% felt 'significantly happier' and 64% reported sleeping better. Critics, however, argue that minimalism is often a luxury of the wealthy — people struggling financially may not have the option to ‘declutter’ their lives because they already own little. Others point out that social media has turned minimalism into an aesthetic trend rather than a meaningful philosophy."

subQuestions.text: Explain briefly (in English) what the phrase "luxury of the wealthy" means in this context.
subQuestions.sampleAnswer: It means that minimalism is an option only for people who already have enough money — they can choose to own less. Those struggling financially don't have that choice because they already have very little.

subQuestions.text: Assess these statements.

statements.text: The text states that 64% of participants slept better after adopting minimalism.
According to the text, minimalism has universal benefits regardless of income level.
The author presents minimalism in a completely positive light.
Social media's role in minimalism is presented as problematic by critics.

subQuestions.text: Summarise the critics' main argument against minimalism in one sentence.
subQuestions.sampleAnswer: Critics argue that minimalism is a privilege of the wealthy and has been reduced to a social media aesthetic rather than a meaningful lifestyle choice.

explanation: Tekst wyważony (pro + contra). Krytycy: klasowy przywilej + estetyka mediów społecznościowych. 78% happier, 64% better sleep.

content.context:
Read the text:
"Overtourism has become a defining issue for European cities. Venice introduced a €5 entry fee for day-trippers in 2024, while Barcelona plans to completely phase out short-term holiday rentals by 2028. Residents of these cities complain that mass tourism has driven up rents, displaced locals, and transformed historic neighbourhoods into open-air theme parks. On the other hand, tourism accounts for roughly 12% of the EU's GDP and supports nearly 24 million jobs. Striking a balance is proving difficult — some argue that higher prices simply shift tourism to other, less-prepared destinations, rather than reducing it overall."

subQuestions.text: What action has Venice taken?

options.text: Introduced an entry fee for day-trippers

subQuestions.text: Tourism's role in the EU economy:
The critique of the higher-price approach is that:

So, my favourite hobby at the moment is urban sketching — which basically means going out into the city and drawing what you see around you. Buildings, cafes, people waiting for the bus, that kind of thing. I started doing it about two years ago when a friend lent me a really nice sketchbook for my birthday. I thought, well, why not give it a try?

At first I was terrible, honestly. My lines were messy and nothing looked the way I wanted it to. But I kept practising, and after a few months I actually started to enjoy it more because I could see myself improving. That's the best feeling, you know?

What I love most about it is that you don't need much — just a pen, a sketchbook, and somewhere interesting to sit. I usually go out on Saturday mornings, before the city gets too crowded. My favourite spot is this old market square near my house. There's always something different happening there.

I've also joined a small local group of sketchers. We meet up once a month and draw together, then share what we made. It's really nice because everyone is super supportive — nobody judges your work. I've even posted a few of my drawings on Instagram and got some really positive comments, which was unexpected but really motivating. I'd definitely recommend trying urban sketching to anyone who thinks they're not artistic — because honestly, that's exactly how I felt at the beginning.
